words: This conversation with CLARA GROVE took place at her home in Moscow, Idaho,
on May 11, 1976. Interviewer: SAM SCHRAGER.
CLARA GROVE: Someone would come and ask where John Smith lives, and they would
say, "Well, right over there on the other side of the creek. Do you
see that cow staked out there? That's Moe's cow. and he stakes it
out there day, and you go past Moe's cow, and then you'll go past
the first log house, and the second log house that you come to is
John Smith." But that's the way people were directed in that day
by Moe's cow. So they called it Moscow. Now, whether there is any
truth in that, I don't know. But, anyway, I'm sure you'll be help
ful by remembering about a boy. And the other thing I wanted to tell;
now, do you think just gently and friendly without any fists, we
could talk politics a little bit? (Chuckles)

speaker: SS
words: Sure, if you want to.

speaker: CG
words: Well, it's not that I'm so about politics. My father was a

speaker: CG
words: politician first, and if there was any time left, he'd be a father,

speaker: CG
words: but he was a politician first always. And, I grew up politics,

speaker: CG
words: and until just the last very few years, I have been very active in

speaker: CG
words: politics, but my mind doesnt carry anything over. I get something

speaker: CG
words: and it deserts me, and I would like a little bit of information.

speaker: CG
words: Now, what is it? Four elections we have coming on? I think it is.

speaker: SS
words: In the primaries?

speaker: CG
words: Well, Primary or something, and I don't know how we have four or

speaker: CG
words: what they're about.

speaker: SS
words: You know the way I understand what it is— (Machine shut off)

speaker: CG
words: — he was so known or that the people so much wanted him or anything,

speaker: CG
words: but he was elected on the slogan, "The way to reduce taxes, is to

speaker: CG
words: spend less money." They elected him governor and it is said that

speaker: CG
words: North Dakota never had so prosperous a time as while he was governor.

speaker: CG
words: And, he reduced taxes. Well, I think that any man who can grasp the

speaker: CG
words: situation in this country to the extent that he can reduce throwmgA

speaker: CG
words: money away, should be in the White House regardless of politics or

speaker: CG
words: anything else. But, I just haven't known anything about any of these

speaker: CG
words: men. And I think that voting is such an obligation that since people

speaker: CG
words: are becoming so negligent about it, so refusing to vote, that if no

speaker: CG
words: thing else, there should be a cash or jail for one mentally and

speaker: CG
words: physically capable of voting and not vot ., I think they should be

speaker: CG
words: compelled to vote, and vote as they please. I don't know how it would

speaker: CG
words: come out, but in my idea that's the fair way to do it. So, I am glad

speaker: CG
words: to hear anything about these men.

speaker: SS
words: Thereks one more thing I could say about—

speaker: CG
words: — going to progress, now, that's certain. And the way that the rural

speaker: CG
words: has been and is being administered, is death to our country, if it

speaker: CG
words: keeps on. To me, anybody,„ you, me or anybody else would be better

speaker: CG
words: than Ford. I wouldn't care who it was. They couldn't be any worse.

speaker: SS
words: Why do you feel that way?

speaker: CG
words: Well, so far as any religious belief is concerned, I think that's

speaker: CG
words: all hooey. I think it's assumed for effect. It may not be. It may

speaker: CG
words: be better than what I have in that line, but it doesn't impress me

speaker: CG
words: as being sincere. (sounds as though the recorder shuts off inter

speaker: CG
words: mittently) Asked for a donation of money and so this A said, "Are

speaker: CG
words: you hungry?" And, B said, "Yes, I'm hungry." He said, "Well, I'll

speaker: CG
words: gladly take you to a meal.A Come on in and I'll get you a meal."

speaker: CG
words: The man said, "No, I don't want a meal, I want money." And A said,

speaker: CG
words: "Well, if you're hungry, why do you want money instead of food?" He

speaker: CG
words: said, "Because I'm a Communist. If I can get a dollar, I'll buy one

speaker: CG
words: loaf of yesterday's bread and get along with that and give the rest

speaker: CG
words: of the money to the Communist party." I have seen a man do stunts

speaker: CG
words: just like that, so I know something about the Communist Party. And

speaker: CG
words: they work so slowly, so methodically. I'm going to be ninety-seven

speaker: CG
words: now in just a couple of days, and way back when I was in my early

speaker: CG
words: twenties a minister of the gospel, who was touring and speaking in

speaker: CG
words: schoolhouses or where ever he could get a group, say, "And what is to

speaker: CG
words: prevent the Russians coming across that very narrow gulf of water

speaker: CG
words: between Russia and the United States and taking over United States?"

speaker: CG
words: He said, "What's to prevent that?" Well, I'm ninety-seven now, and

speaker: CG
words: that's when I was in my early twenties that that man saW that. No

speaker: CG
words: one else that I ever heard of had heard of such a thing at that time.

speaker: CG
words: Well, they haven necessarily come across that strait^mostly come

speaker: CG
words: by air, with our money. In my opinion, they're getting very close to

speaker: CG
words: the place where they'll say,"Take down your flag."

speaker: SS
words: I'd like to ask you something. You just said a little while ago that

speaker: SS
words: when you mentioned one religion; you mentioned that the Communists

speaker: SS
words: said that if Christians had been as strong in their Christianity they

speaker: SS
words: could have had one religion and one faith. Now, I'm wondering: In

speaker: SS
words: the early days, was Christianity more that way? Within the towns

speaker: SS
words: and the communities, was it more one faith than it is now?

speaker: CG
words: Well, at that time, I think Christianity depended on church member

speaker: CG
words: ship as much as it does now, but, I do think that the nation, as a

speaker: CG
words: whole, or at least any part of it in which I was, and I circulated

speaker: CG
words: around quite a little, that any part of it lived nearer to the con

speaker: CG
words: cept as given us in the scripture than they have through the years

speaker: CG
words: since. That gradually, from the time I was a child til right now,

speaker: CG
words: we have gradually drifted away from the scripture to man-made rules

speaker: CG
words: only. And that the Communists have dominated the world more than

speaker: CG
words: any other concept of what they are after.

speaker: SS
words: When you say, living closer to the scripture what do you mean

speaker: SS
words: by that? How did that make people live then?

speaker: CG
words: Well, we didn't have rape and murder and burglary as the common diet.

speaker: CG
words: There was so little of it, that it was almost unknown. We never had

speaker: CG
words: prisons that were over full in the town where I lived for some years

speaker: CG
words: in early life before I was a teenager and a little while afterwards.

speaker: CG
words: And there were times when there was nobody in our jail. And there

speaker: CG
words: were times that there would be one or two or possibly three for a

speaker: CG
words: little while. But a jail was just, well, something you had because

speaker: CG
words: it was part of the city setup; not that you really needed it for any

speaker: CG
words: thing of that kind. And as for rape, it was practically unknown.

speaker: CG
words: And as for murder, there were so few that you scarcely knew the word.

speaker: CG
words: And as for burglary, there was very little of it. Very little. The

speaker: CG
words: moral of the people was much higher than it is now. And as for peo

speaker: CG
words: ple living together unmarried; in our town— my father worked on the

speaker: CG
words: railroad— and in that way I knew some railroad families that I

speaker: CG
words: wouldn't have known otherwise— One of them was a family named Cooper

speaker: CG
words: and Mr. Cooper was much older than his wife and they had two boys

speaker: CG
words: and a baby girl. At the time that this Mr. Cooper applied for his

speaker: CG
words: Civil War pension—well, he hadn't thought that they were going to

speaker: CG
words: look up his record all his life before they'd grant a pension— which

speaker: CG
words: of course they do— and they found at that time that he had a wife

speaker: CG
words: living in the East. Well, they wrote to him that since he had a wife

speaker: CG
words: in the East who was living, and he claimed a wife here, that no pen

speaker: CG
words: sion would be granted. To him, that was absolutely penitentiary.

speaker: CG
words: No way 'round. The judge wouldn't judge anything else and they would

speaker: CG
words: n't fail to pick him up. And he read his letter and he just simply

speaker: CG
words: got out of town. Nobody knew where he went or just exactly when.

speaker: CG
words: Well, there was this young wife with— the older boy was, I think

speaker: CG
words: about eleven, something like that, and then a younger boy and the

speaker: CG
words: baby girl. Well, there she was and there was no welfare at that

speaker: CG
words: time; there she was without any support at all. Well, I don't know

speaker: CG
words: how she got along. I do know that the older boy was working on a

speaker: CG
words: ranch and broke his arm and had to be taken home with a broken arm.

speaker: CG
words: But to get a doctor to set it probably wouldn't take more than five

speaker: CG
words: dollars all told. And there was no hospital; he would be at home,

speaker: CG
words: of course. So that it wasn't like it is now, at all. But that was

speaker: CG
words: punishable, that was penitentary, and this man knew that immediately

speaker: CG
words: he would be picked up and he would be judged, and he would be put in

speaker: CG
words: the peniteniary. And they never heard of him again in the town.

speaker: SS
words: You talk about his wife, his young wife, it makes me wonder— with

speaker: SS
words: no welfare what a woman who had been abandoned by a husband or had

speaker: SS
words: no husband but children, her husband, say, even had died, what she

speaker: SS
words: could— What her situation was. Was she in dire straits?

speaker: CG
words: Apparently not.

speaker: SS
words: I don't mean just her, but kind of in general, when women found them

speaker: SS
words: selves in this kind of situation.

speaker: CG
words: Well, there was county aid. That was entirely a personal individual

speaker: CG
words: matter. I presume that she applied for county aid, in which the Coun

speaker: CG
words: ty Commissioners would take her circumstance and judge her circum

speaker: CG
words: stance on her circumstance and make their decision as to what county

speaker: CG
words: aid get. It had no relation to anything former or coming, it

speaker: CG
words: was at the moment, that if someone wanted county aid then the County

speaker: CG
words: Commissioners ruled on that case as they saw it. And, I suppose she

speaker: CG
words: got county aid. I don't know how much. It wouldn't be any great

speaker: CG
words: sum, but they owned their home. And, of course, she would own it

speaker: CG
words: now. And, well, I suppose that thirty-five dollars a month would

speaker: CG
words: have been sufficient to keep them. It probably would have been.

speaker: SS
words: What kind of prospects would a woman in that kind of situation— not

speaker: SS
words: only her, but when that would happen to people— what kind of pros

speaker: SS
words: pects would they have? If you had children and in a world, where I

speaker: SS
words: take it it wasn't so easy to make a living, very difficult, would

speaker: SS
words: she have to try to remarry? Or would women be able to go it on their

speaker: SS
words: own successfully?

speaker: CG
words: It is very probable in this case that she would remarry. Now, I do

speaker: CG
words: not know about her following this event. But, for one thing, I was

speaker: CG
words: too young at the time to have any object in pursuing it.

speaker: SS
words: I'm really thinking in general, not just for this particular case.

speaker: SS
words: I'm wondering how society took care of people who were in

speaker: CG
words: Remarriage was very probably for any one in that situation. Very

speaker: CG
words: probable. Because, the country was new enough that there was no short

speaker: CG
words: age of men, and there would be, say, a farmer who would be so glad to

speaker: CG
words: have a housekeeper that he would gladly marry her with her children

speaker: CG
words: in order to have a housekeeper, rather than do his own cooking after

speaker: CG
words: he'd worked in the field all day. So that marriage was very probable

speaker: CG
words: for any such woman. And this woman was still quite young. Her hus

speaker: CG
words: band ha been much older than she was. But that was the only case of

speaker: CG
words: that that I knew of, and that was because they were railroad peoplemy

speaker: CG
words: father was, and I had known the family before this happened, is

speaker: CG
words: how I knew about it.

speaker: SS
words: Were railroad people more— people that were just working on the rail

speaker: SS
words: road, were they more likely to move around from place to place?

speaker: CG
words: No. No, they were not rail men. The roundhouse, it was a division

speaker: CG
words: point, and there was a two-stall roundhouse where the engines were

speaker: CG
words: cleaned and serviced. And these were settlers in the town. They

speaker: CG
words: might be there all their lives, working in the roundhouse or on the

speaker: CG
words: section, one or the other.

speaker: SS
words: Would they be likely to hold a little land outside of town as well,

speaker: SS
words: or were they towns people?

speaker: CG
words: They were just people, just like the rest of them.

speaker: SS
words: And they wouldn't be farming as well?

speaker: CG
words: No. No, they weren't trying to do anything else. My father— well

speaker: CG
words: finally there were seven children, but the seventh one came ten yearseleven

speaker: CG
words: years gap between the one above and the last one. And at this

speaker: CG
words: time there were six children,although the oldest one was mostly away

speaker: CG
words: on his own*at this time. But, anyway, there were the five of us there

speaker: CG
words: and Father and Mother, and we paid the enormous sum of eight dollars

speaker: CG
words: a month for a three bedroom house, a good house, in a good location.

speaker: CG
words: We paid eight dollars a month out of that forty. Well, there was

speaker: CG
words: thirty-two dollars for food and clothing and school and anything else

speaker: CG
words: that might come up. We got along. And at times, each of the older

speaker: CG
words: brothers worked for some time in the roundhouse. Father's work was

speaker: CG
words: solely in the roundhouse.

speaker: SS
words: Were they expected to contribute their wages to the family?

speaker: CG
words: No, they were not expected to, any more than anyone else would be.

speaker: CG
words: That is, you mean to his family?

speaker: SS
words: Yes. Your brothers, before they were married, when they worked out,

speaker: SS
words: did they bring back their earnings to help your father and mother out?

speaker: CG
words: They did or did not, as they chose. But there was law on the books

speaker: CG
words: that the father could collect the wages and if he wanted the wages

speaker: CG
words: and did not collect it from the boy, it might be that whoever paid

speaker: CG
words: it would have to pay it to the father again, because he was entitled

speaker: CG
words: until the boy was twenty-one to the wages. But, usually, he did not

speaker: CG
words: collect it. And, I remember a boy that I played around with while I

speaker: CG
words: was teaching saying that his dad gave him his time til he would be

speaker: CG
words: twenty-one. Well, that was a gift from the father. He gave over his

speaker: CG
words: right to the son's wages. But that was done frequently, that the

speaker: CG
words: father would make the son a present of whatever he earned til he was

speaker: CG
words: twenty-one.

speaker: SS
words: Would this be decided by the father's own attitude, or would it be

speaker: SS
words: more like a natural situation?

speaker: CG
words: It would be more his own mental idea of whether he or the boy should

speaker: CG
words: have it. And each one had his own idea. The law was very clear on

speaker: CG
words: the matter, so there was no trouble there. It was quite a different

speaker: CG
words: time than it is now.

speaker: SS
words: I'm fascinated by what it was like then. (Chuckles)

speaker: CG
words: Well, it was like this; probably you didn't even have a lock on your

speaker: CG
words: door. You didn't need it. In the hot weather,Awe slept with the

speaker: CG
words: door open all night. We didn't need a lock, nobody was coming in

speaker: CG
words: if he had no business to. And, there came a time, after a while,

speaker: CG
words: that hard times struck the country. We didn't call it a recession,

speaker: CG
words: or anything of that sort, it was hard times. And we knew what

speaker: CG
words: that meant. There would be a string of men walking the rails and

speaker: CG
words: ties, and our house was right by the railroad, and they would come

speaker: CG
words: to the kitchen door and ask for something to eat, and some of them

speaker: CG
words: asked if they might do some work for something to eat. And some of

speaker: CG
words: them after they had eaten would ask if they could do any work. And

speaker: CG
words: some of them didn't say anything about any work. And Mother fed evGROVE

speaker: CG
words: ery one of them that came and asked. And, we didn't just hand 'em

speaker: CG
words: out something, she brought them in and set them at the table; fixed

speaker: CG
words: 'em a meal. And someone said to me, "Uh-huh, your mother's going

speaker: CG
words: to be killed at that some day. You'll find it out, too. So, I run

speaker: CG
words: and told Mother, and Mother said, "There is no man that's as dangeroug

speaker: CG
words: as a hungry man."

speaker: CG
words: Well, none of them was ever in any way im

speaker: CG
words: polite or anything. He was merely grateful and expressed his thank

speaker: CG
words: fulness for his food. And, some of them were hobos. We knew the

speaker: CG
words: difference between a hobo and a businessman when we saw them coming.

speaker: CG
words: But, there were some of them were hobos. And some of them were sim

speaker: CG
words: ply businessmen in their business clothes, just as they had sat in

speaker: CG
words: their offices until there was no way to sit there any longer. And

speaker: CG
words: there was nothing to do; nothing to be had, and they would venture

speaker: CG
words: forth and see if there was any place that there was something. And,

speaker: CG
words: there was another time of hard times after that, and it was a little

speaker: CG
words: bit different. I was in Des Moines, Iowa for a while at that time

speaker: CG
words: in an apartment there for a few days, and a man came to the door,

speaker: CG
words: he was a nicely dressed businessman, and he asked me if he could

speaker: CG
words: have something to eat. Well, a woman alone doesn't usually have very

speaker: CG
words: much to eat right around, but anyway, I got something and gave it to

speaker: CG
words: him. And he said, "Do you have any coffee?" I said, "No, I don't,"

speaker: CG
words: I said, "I never drink it, and I don't have any in the house." But

speaker: CG
words: he wanted a cup of coffee so badly. But he evidently had been a pro

speaker: CG
words: sperous businessman, and business just was failing all over the coun

speaker: CG
words: try; and there they were. And, at one time, oh, it was years later,

speaker: CG
words: I was living in a cabin on a farm where other people lived, and the

speaker: CG
words: other people were gone, and a man came to the door one day and want

speaker: CG
words: ed something to eat. Well, he was sort of a strange acting

speaker: CG
words: but, I always remembered what Mother said about a hungry man being

speaker: CG
words: the dangerous one, so I told him, Yes," I'd get home something to eat

speaker: CG
words: And he sat there shoveling it down with his knife and fork as fast

speaker: CG
words: as he could eat, and now and then he'd say, "A picture machine." Well, at that

speaker: CG
words: time picture machine was very new. It wasn't developed at all, as

speaker: CG
words: it has been later; and I presumed that he had cast his money into a

speaker: CG
words: picture venture had failed, and that it had turned his mind a

speaker: CG
words: little. But he ate his meal still muttering to himself about a pic

speaker: CG
words: ture machine and he left. I was glad he left, because it was very

speaker: CG
words: apparent that his mind wasn't right and I was alone on the whole farm.

speaker: CG
words: And later yet, in '27, when I came to Moscow there were hard times.

speaker: CG
words: Did you realize that?

speaker: SS
words: I heard about that.

speaker: CG
words: 1927. Well, now, unless they have so much money, a certain amount of

speaker: CG
words: money, they can't go to University, they think. At that time

speaker: CG
words: they went and took a chance. I was on the campus one day; every girl

speaker: CG
words: that I saw on the campus either had a hole in the heel of her stock

speaker: CG
words: ing or she had her stocking pulled down into her shoe to cover the

speaker: CG
words: hole! Every girl that I met! (Chuckles) That's the way they went

speaker: CG
words: then! Now, if she can't have two or three formals, she just ain't a

speaker: CG
words: going, that's all!! Which is quite different.

speaker: SS
words: 1 would like to ask you a little bit about, more, about marriage in

speaker: SS
words: those days. Since we've talked about it, I've read some things about

speaker: SS
words: it and I'm very interested in understanding better the differences

speaker: SS
words: in opinion now and then people had. Now, one thing is, I have the

speaker: SS
words: idea that there was a lot of— more stress on marriage then, of get

speaker: SS
words: ting married than there is nowdays. That when girls grew up, they

words: just about had to get married if they were going to have a place in

speaker: SS
words: their communities. That's the idea I get. Is that true, do you think?

speaker: SS
words: Were there many women who didn't marry?

speaker: CG
words: It used to be that there would be one "shotgun marriage" in one place

speaker: CG
words: in a long time, before there would ever be another. Or, perhaps you

speaker: CG
words: wouldn't be there long enough that you would ever hear of another.

speaker: CG
words: They were very uncommon. And, And, as for in the University— see

speaker: CG
words: my daughter was in '27 to '31, four years there— and the Dean of

speaker: CG
words: Women was gone on a trip to the South, and there—(End Tape A)

speaker: CG
words: "I don't have any trouble at all with my girls in that way. I'm

speaker: CG
words: strict with them, and I don't have any such trouble." And, after

speaker: CG
words: being gone a couple of weeks she came back and one of the women who

speaker: CG
words: had been taking her place as Dean rushed over and she said, "Oh,

speaker: CG
words: Dean French, there are five girls here who are pregnant!" Dean

speaker: CG
words: French had supposed there were none! But, in the whole University,

speaker: CG
words: there were these five. And later, when I was cooking on the campus,

speaker: CG
words: and that would be after '31, be after '33, to be exact, while I was

speaker: CG
words: cooking on the campus there were four boys in the kitchen with me as

speaker: CG
words: ccmmnon and one of them was a great favorite with me. A six-footer,

speaker: CG
words: handsome youngster, and jovial and just full of pleasantries, and I

speaker: CG
words: liked him so much. I was very fond of him. And at the place where

speaker: CG
words: he lodged, he had an upstairs room, and the stairway went up, oh,

speaker: CG
words: sort of semiprivately to those who were on the second floor, and the

speaker: CG
words: woman with whom he lodged got the impression that a girl was coming

speaker: CG
words: in by night, into this boy's room. So, she waited until finally,

speaker: CG
words: she knew that a girl had been up there for the night. She went up

speaker: CG
words: to the room and she picked up the evidence that the girl had been

speaker: CG
words: there. She hadn't quite taken all her clothes away with her. Well,

speaker: CG
words: she picked up the garment and took it to the Dean of Men. The Dean

speaker: CG
words: of Men called this boy in and explained to him that this was the Uni

speaker: CG
words: versity and these things were not tolerated, and that if he and the

speaker: CG
words: girl, both, would just quietyly withdraw, without any furor, without

speaker: CG
words: saying anything to anybody, just leave, that they would be allowed

speaker: CG
words: to leave in that way. Well, when— I'm not going to say his real

speaker: CG
words: name, I'm going to call him Joe—Joe didn't come for his work one

speaker: CG
words: noon, and I said to his cousin, who also was in the crew, "Where is

speaker: CG
words: Joe?" "Oh, he's around somewhere." I said, "Stop it, I want to

speaker: CG
words: know where Joe is. Now you tell me where Joe is." He said, "He's

speaker: CG
words: in his room." Well, I went over to his room as soon as my noon work

speaker: CG
words: was done up, which wasn't long, and Joe's father— I never can for

speaker: CG
words: get how he looked— he sat there with his hands hanging between his

speaker: CG
words: knees and his head hanging. Just the picture of despair. And Joe

speaker: CG
words: the most wretched looking youngster you would ever think of seeing,

speaker: CG
words: was busy packing his trunk. And I said to the Father, "I just had to

speaker: CG
words: come over and see Joe. I think a lot of that boy. And his father

speaker: CG
words: said, "We think a lot of him, too." He was taking the boy and the

speaker: CG
words: girl back to the Father's home, since they had no place else to go.

speaker: CG
words: Well, they were married after they got back there. But here was his

speaker: CG
words: university career interrupted. He couldn't get a degree.

speaker: SS
words: Did he ever go back?

words: are cases in which a woman can take on that and live with it, and

speaker: CG
words: be gloriously happy; but they're so rare. They just happen once in

speaker: CG
words: a lifetime. But Carl was a mighty good boy. We had another very

speaker: CG
words: good boy who married the school teacher, way off, quite a distance

speaker: CG
words: from us where he homesteaded. I met her, and I think they had two

speaker: CG
words: children at the time I met her. But, anyway, later I heard that she

speaker: CG
words: had gotten to looking so seedy, with never getting anything new or

speaker: CG
words: not keeping herself up, that Matt gave her a hundred dollars and he

speaker: CG
words: said, "Now, you go to town and fix yourself up with new clothes."

speaker: CG
words: Well, she went to town, alright enough, no question, she went to

speaker: CG
words: town— she came home with a hundred turkey poults, from that hun

speaker: CG
words: dred dollars! (Chuckles) Incredible as it seems, I had a Christ

speaker: CG
words: mas card from Matt two years ago, and he was in a hospital at the

speaker: CG
words: time, I don't know anything more about them than that.

speaker: SS
words: Well, was she more practical than he was? Is that the moral of that

speaker: SS
words: story?

speaker: CG
words: I don't think it was practical altogether, I think it was just not

speaker: CG
words: laying the proposition out and selecting what she should out

speaker: CG
words: of it. Now, if she wanted to get ten or twenty poults, all right,

speaker: CG
words: but she had no right to spend that hundred dollars on turkey . And

speaker: CG
words: I can imagine how Matt would feel. He was such a splendid young man.

speaker: CG
words: And, I heard that he got to drinking very badly. Well, I suppose

speaker: CG
words: he was trying to hide his unhappiness; as though liquor would do it.

speaker: SS
words: I was going to ask you that, too. Do you think that that is often

speaker: SS
words: the reason why a man would start to drink? To hide his unhappiness?

speaker: SS
words: Or to escape from his frustrations, or disappointments in life?

speaker: CG
words: I think that his unhappiness and disappointment have befuddled his

speaker: CG
words: mind to a little extent. That it doesn't function as it should to

speaker: CG
words: lay the proposition out and work the whole thing mathematically as

speaker: CG
words: to whether he does or doesn't do this, that, or the othere But I

speaker: CG
words: think he isn't quite as capable of doing that, but I do think that

speaker: CG
words: unhappiness, not only from marriage, but business or ill health or

speaker: CG
words: various things, they do start drinkingfthinking it will stop that.

speaker: CG
words: And, I think that aside from that, quite a bit of it is that the

speaker: CG
words: bunch is drinking and he doesn't have grit enough — lemonade for

speaker: CG
words: mine— he doesn't have the grit to do it. I read of a Congressional

speaker: CG
words: the Congressmen were having a party, and one of them said, to

speaker: CG
words: another one, "As you see, I just have orange juice, He said,

speaker: CG
words: "Some people think it's strange, but I never take anything more than

speaker: CG
words: a fruit juice." And the other one said, "Well, that's what I have,

speaker: CG
words: you notice." He said, "I never drink anything else." So, it

speaker: CG
words: can be done.

speaker: CG
words: These were two men in the United States Congress. It

speaker: CG
words: can be done, even if the rest of the bunch has something else. But

speaker: CG
words: there aren't so many who have the grit to do it. Just the same as,

speaker: CG
words: well, on St. Patrick's Day, some people put on a green tie and others

speaker: CG
words: wouldn't think of being that conspicuous. And they're afraid of not

speaker: CG
words: getting in with the bunch if they don't do as the bunch does. Never

speaker: CG
words: thinking that maybe some of the bunch would like to do something else,

speaker: CG
words: too. There are quite a number of reasons, and at the present time

speaker: CG
words: there are lots of children who are not more than ten years old who

speaker: CG
words: are confirmed alcoholics. I got that fact from a factual source,

speaker: CG
words: just recently. Isn't that something awful? A ten year old a chronic

speaker: CG
words: alcoholic! Well, I suppose, that a child starts drinking because

speaker: CG
words: Dad and Mother drink. Or, that he doesn't know any better. He has

speaker: CG
words: no knowledge what the consequences can be. So, if other people

speaker: CG
words: are drinking it, well, so does he. The amount that is consumed—

speaker: CG
words: it's stupenduous. The money spent on it is enough to run the nation

speaker: CG
words: entirely. That is, without throwing it to nations that don't want

speaker: CG
words: it, or are using it to fight us, or something of that kind.
